name: Danvers 126
image_alt: Blunt-tipped deep orange carrots just pulled from soil
description: A classic American heirloom carrot with a blunt tip, broad shoulders, and deep orange flesh throughout. More tolerant of heavy or clay soils than most carrots, and excellent for long-term storage.
history: Developed in Danvers, Massachusetts in the 1870s and selected over decades for reliability and storage quality. Became the standard commercial carrot of the late 19th century and remains one of the most widely grown heirlooms.

good.reason: Compatible root depths, efficient use of space
Onion fly and carrot fly deter each other

seasonal_quirks: Flavor sweetens dramatically after frost as starches convert to sugars. Can be left in ground through winter in mild climates. Needs consistent moisture for crack-free roots.
harvest_cues: Shoulders should be 3/4 inch or more across. Brush away soil to check root diameter. Tops of roots visible at soil surface. 70–80 days from seeding.
variety_notes: Shorter and blunter than Imperator types — better for heavy soils where long carrots fork or struggle. Sweet and tender when young, stores well when mature.
preservation: Stores in cold, moist conditions for 4–6 months. Blanch and freeze. Excellent pickled.
